concepts.title: From pixels to features — the CV problem

beats.text: A digital image is just a grid of numbers, with the gap between those numbers and a label being the whole problem.

visual.sub: The semantic gap in computer vision

beats.text: A modest 224 by 224 RGB photo holds 150,528 raw values, and none of them tells you a cat is in the frame.
Vision is supervised learning on a pixel grid: we fit a function from the image to a label by minimizing a loss.
For decades the answer was hand-engineered features like SIFT and HOG, which worked within limits but needed a human to guess what mattered.
Deep learning instead learns the feature hierarchy from data: edges in early layers, textures in the middle, whole objects deep down.
A brightness shift moves every pixel value yet keeps the scene the same, which is exactly why raw-pixel search fails.
The architecture that closed the semantic gap was the convolutional neural network, sliding one small learnable kernel across the whole image.

visual.sub: Locality and translation equivariance as built-in priors

beats.text: ImageNet gave the field a shared yardstick: roughly 1.2 million labelled images across 1000 categories.
The 2012 inflection point was AlexNet, which cut top-5 error from about 26 percent to about 16 percent in one stroke.
ResNet was the decisive jump, adding the block's input back to its output so each block learns only a residual correction.
That single skip connection enabled 152-layer networks, won ImageNet 2015, and dropped top-5 error to about 3.6 percent.
Convolution did not lose; it became one well-understood tool, still winning where data is scarce or latency matters.
The Vision Transformer turns an image into a sequence of tokens and then changes almost nothing else about the Transformer.
Cut a 224 by 224 image into 16 by 16 patches and you get a 14 by 14 grid, which is 196 patches.

visual.label: patches from a 224x224 image at P=16

beats.text: Each raw patch is flattened and projected by one learned matrix, with a CLS token and positional embeddings prepended.
ViT gains a global receptive field from layer one, where a CNN needs many layers to relate distant regions.
The price is that ViT is data-hungry: it underperforms a ResNet on ImageNet-1k but overtakes it after pre-training on 303 million images.
Self-attention costs grow with the square of the patch count, so halving the patch size roughly 16 times the attention compute.
Classification asks what is in the image; most real tasks demand what, and where, and the answer's shape defines the task family.
Detection predicts a box and class per instance, while segmentation labels pixels, either as regions, instances, or both.
Detection ran from two-stage region proposers to single-shot YOLO detectors and now to set-prediction transformers like DETR.
The universal scoring currency is Intersection over Union: the overlap of two boxes divided by their combined area.
A detection is conventionally accepted when IoU is at least 0.5, with stricter benchmarks averaging over thresholds up to 0.95.
The 2026 frontier is open-vocabulary perception: SAM segments from a prompt and Grounding DINO detects objects named by free text.
CLIP learns from the open web instead of a fixed label list, training on roughly 400 million image and caption pairs.
Two encoders that never share weights map an image and a caption into one shared embedding space.
Once both outputs are normalized, cosine similarity measures the angle between them, high when they describe the same thing.

visual.note: Cosine similarity on the unit sphere

beats.text: A symmetric InfoNCE loss makes the diagonal of the similarity matrix hot and everything off-diagonal cold.
The payoff is zero-shot classification: write each label as a sentence, embed it, and pick the closest to the image.
CLIP matched a fully-supervised ResNet-50 on ImageNet without seeing a single ImageNet training label.
